claim: The Voynich text uses the Sergian translation tradition (6th century Syriac), evidenced by: (a) tak-sa for dynamis (not haylā), (b) <=8 Greek loanwords (40 tokens total, all short transliterations), (c) native Syriac plant names dominate.
evidence_for: Near-total absence of long Greek transliterations in decoded output
Matches Calà and Hawley 2017 'translated' subset (270/477 Sergius entries)
evidence_against: Absence of Greek is also consistent with text not being Syriac at all
Dating the cipher to 6th-century tradition when the MS is 15th-century requires source-copy hypothesis
test: Count Greek-transliteration-compatible skeletons in decoded output; compare against Sergian baseline
test_threshold: <=10 distinct Greek terms and <=60 tokens: consistent. >20 terms: challenged.
tests_remaining: Count Greek-transliteration-compatible skeletons in decoded output; compare against Sergian baseline
reasoning: Dating/tradition claim; hinges on primary hypothesis.
source: Brady Defibaugh (April 2026), A Syriac Pharmaceutical Dispensatory Encoded in the Voynich Manuscript (DANI pipeline v31)
